meta.title: Align Audio and Text for Speech Recognition Model Training
meta.description:
Get repo access at Trelis.com/ADVANCED-audio

Get Trelis All Access (Trelis.com/All-Access)
1. Access all SEVEN Trelis Github Repos (-robotics, -vision, -evals, -fine-tuning, -inference, -voice, -time-series)
2. Support via Github Issues & Trelis' Private Discord
3. Early access to Trelis videos via Discord

Tip: If you subscribe here on YouTube, click the bell to be notified of new vids

Done-for-you Custom Fine-tuning Services
Learn More: https://trelis.com/fine-tuning-services/

Starting a New Project/Venture?
Apply for a Trelis Grant: https://trelis.com/trelis-ai-grants/

Get Trelis AI Tutorials by Email
Subscribe on Substack: https://trelis.substack.com

Video Links:
- Trelis Studio: https://studio.trelis.com
- CTC Alignment: https://github.com/MahmoudAshraf97/ctc-forced-aligner

TIMESTAMPS:
0:00 Introduction to audio-text alignment for model training
1:05 Demo of Audio Alignment interface with two models
2:06 MMS-FA model and commercially-licensed CTC aligner alternative
3:10 Word timestamps enable sentence detection for clean training chunks
4:21 Multi-step alignment process: normalization, emissions, and character probabilities
5:21 Viterbi process calculates most likely path for final alignment
6:44 Trelis Studio data preparation workflow with audio upload
7:45 Realignment process creates clean 20-30 second chunks with sentence boundaries
8:46 Review of resulting dataset with clean chunks and word timestamps
10:01 Fine-tuning Whisper without timestamps causes catastrophic forgetting
12:07 Emissions are character probabilities generated per audio frame
13:09 Wave2Vec models and text normalization process
15:49 Torch Audio forced aligner non-commercial license restriction
18:56 Viterbi method for mapping ground truth text to audio windows
21:54 Emissions model training using unlabeled data approach
23:34 Multiple valid sequences kept during alignment process
25:36 Wave2Vec pre-training uses masked audio prediction
26:36 Conclusion with repository reference at Trelis.com/advanced-audio

transcript.text: This is a video about aligning text and
audio. It's relevant if you're training
models such as texttospech models or
speechtoext models, particularly if you
want to have timestamps within
transcripts you generate, for example,
from whisper. It's also necessary for
certain models that are token based like
QTIs, texttospech, or speechtoext
models. Last of all, if you're doing
data preparation for training voice
models, you often will want to chunk
that data and you would like the chunks
to respect sentence boundaries. Again,
having timestamps, which means being
able to align the audio and the text is
important here if you want to do that.
I'm going to show you a quick interface
just to illustrate what I mean by
alignment and some of the highle knobs
you can turn and choices you might want
to make when doing so. Then I'll show
you how this integrates into a
commercial application with Trellis
Studio. And last of all, I'll cover a
little bit of theory on how voice
alignment works.
Here is a quick demo that I have vibe
coded called audio alignment. And it
shows us how to align using two
different models. It'll give you a feel
for what alignment is and how it works.
It's included in Trellis advanced
transcription or advanced audio
repository which you can find by going
to trellles.com/advanced-
audio.
First thing I'll do here is just upload
some voice. I've got a short recording.
You can maybe listen to a short piece of
it here. The Solar 10.7B is the fastest
uh runner. Sorry, it's the
it's uh this is me recording myself
calling out some words in 2023, some LLM
related words. And for convenience, I
have connected up fireworks so we can
quickly transcribe this. Okay, so I've
transcribed the text here. Normally, I
would make some corrections if I was
going to train on this. Maybe I would
put in some commas, for example, like
this. I'm not going to be too worried
because I mostly just want to align this
with the audio. Now, there are two
models that I have set up here. The
first is MMS- FA. This is a default mod
model from torch audio, although I
understand it's going to be deprecated.
And the second model is a commercially
friend friendly model that's being run
using the CTC aligner package. This one
is uh openly licensed including for
commercial use. So, I'll start off with
the MMSFA and I'll click align. And in
the background, the model is being
loaded and the alignment is happening.
So here's a sense. I'll just play it.
And you can see that what we now have is
the text is aligned with the audio. So
let me play here.
>> A strong it's a version of MRO 7B with
extra layers. Claude 2.1 is the latest
model from Enthropic.
So you can see here we have uh the
alignment of audio and text. And then we
have a list of time stamps for the
words. So we have the starting time
stamp and the end. And given we have
this, if we now do some form of sentence
detection, say using full stops, we
could detect uh sentences. If there were
clean sentences, which there aren't cuz
this is not necessarily clean text. But
if we did have clean text, we would now
also have word timestamps. And with the
word timestamps and the sentence
detection, we could create clean chunks
that we can then use for training. And
I'll show you an illustration of this a
little bit later.
Something to note, I can just run the uh
commercially friendly one here as well.
You'll notice the time it takes here to
run the alignment. And you'll notice
also that if I run it twice, it'll be
faster because the second time we align,
it has already cached the model on my
Mac. This is all running locally and so
it'll be faster. You can of course run
these on GPUs as well.
Something to appreciate is that
alignment models, which I'll describe in
more detail as to how they work, they
operate on normalized text. This means
without punctu punctuation and with all
lowercase. So if you start with text
that has capitalization and you want to
keep that, you will need to first
convert it to normalized text but
maintain a mapping of the original to
the normalized so that you can then
reverse the mapping afterwards to get
back to having uh punctuated capitalized
text. So you'll see if I remove the
preserve original text, this means that
yes, I'll normalize, but I won't reverse
the mapping. And now when I align, I'll
have lost all of my punctuation. And
you'll see here I even lose the 10.7B
because it's just lost in the
normalization. But when I do normalize
and then back reverse the normalization,
I keep all of the text and I get nice uh
clean alignment just like this.
Underneath the hood, when you run
alignment, it's a multi-step process.
You normalize the text. You take the
normalized text. You set it aside. Then
you take the audio and you put it
through a model that will create
emissions.
What it's going to do is it's going to
create a probability of each given
character for every piece of the audio.
So every window of the audio will
predict with a certain probability
either just a blank character or a
character A B C D and it will give that
a probability. This is called emissions.
So you'll have an emission of
probabilities of the different
characters. That's typically how it
works. Not tokens, but characters.
Now that you have these probabilities at
each time stamp of your audio, you will
then be able to compare. Well, you'll be
able to now put them through what's
called a vertebrae process and you will
calculate the most likely path. And that
most likely path will give you a final
estimate of well it'll give you your
final call on what the aligned text is.
If you didn't follow that, don't worry
because I'll go through it in a bit more
detail. But suffice to say, you take the
audio, the audio will produce using a
neural network. It will produce
emissions which are probabilities of
each character. And when you have the
probability of each character or spaces
for each position, you will then run
those through uh a vertib process which
is going to give you the most likely
sequence. You won't just take the most
likely character at every position
because then they won't be consistent
with each other. What you'll do is
you'll find the most likely total path
and that will give you your final
alignment. Okay, so this is um how to
align. I will go through the code just
in a moment uh showing you how the
different steps and also the specific
models that are chosen. But yeah,
suffice to say, I checked through a lot
of models and unfortunately a lot of the
default ones that are used are not
permissively licensed. Uh, including
this torch audio default, which I think
is going to be deprecated.
Now, as an example of where this is used
in practice from Trellis Studio, I want
to just show you data preparation. A
very quick data preparation I'm going to
do here. I'm going to upload uh some
audio.
I'm going to transcribe the audio.
Again, this uses fireworks in the
background. And now, typically, I would
review the transcripts. I would maybe
make some corrections uh to some of the
words here. I won't worry about that too
much. You can see that when I
transcribe, I've got these 30-second
chunks, but they're not neat chunks
because they finish in the middle of a
sentence here and here. So, what I'd
really like to do is when my data is
cleaned and corrected, I'd like to
realign it so that I just have clean
chunks of about 20 to 30 seconds that
start and finish with clean sentence
boundaries. Uh, cuz this is going to
give me better data for when it comes to
training.
So, I'll go back to data prep here and
I'm going to run data processing. And
the data processing is doing what I
showed already in this quick demo. It's
aligning all of the audio. It's getting
all of the timestamps and given the
timestamps and looking at sentence
boundaries with punctuation, we can then
create 30 second 20 to 30 second chunks
of audio that are going to be neatly
prepared uh for running tra training.
So here you can see we have um the
chunking underway and the data set is
created. Looks like there will be nine
uh total chunks of about 20 to 30
seconds.
And we can even use this for training if
we like or we can just view the data
set. I'm going to give it a moment uh to
show up. If I wanted to use for
training, I would just copy in the
training data set name. I could just
omit if I've no validation set and then
decide to train one of the Whisper
models here and later deploy it with
endpoints. But I don't want to get too
distracted because I'm focused on
alignment here.
And we should have a look at the data
set now. Yep. And you can see that
there's now a series of chunks. There's
also the word timestamps available here.
And you can see hopefully each chunk is
going to be clean and finishing at a
neat sentence boundary because I've been
able to just take the first 20 to 30
seconds.
And then yeah, you can see the same for
other text uh such as here. Now, in
certain cases, because I'm targeting
between 20 and 30 seconds, if there is
no sentence that ends between 20 and 30
seconds, you can still get a
discontinuous sentence like that. Uh but
generally, we're going to be able to get
these nice and consistent chunk lengths.
And here, all I'm doing is creating neat
sentence respecting chunks. But if
you're training a model like QTI's
speechto text model, you actually need
timestamps. Or if you're training
whisper with timestamps, which I hope to
show later in another video, you need
explicit time stamps for the words. And
you need to actually inject timestamp
tokens in the middle of these uh
segments or at least at the start and
the end so that the model learns how to
time stamp. And so this is how models
are trained to have a sense of time
stamp. And you should know that whisper
models actually are trained with
timestamps within their data.
And that is why you can decide to
inference with or without time stamps
when you use Whisper. Now, if you just
fine-tune, it will often remember its
ability to time stamp. So, you can often
fine-tune without time stamp data. But
if you're training a large amount and
you're going to move the weight
significantly, then probably you want to
include timestamp data within your data
set if you want to make sure the model
doesn't do catastrophic forgetting.
So, that's a quick illustration of why
uh timestamping might be useful. I'm
going to show you some code now for the
alignment. This is back in the advanced
transcription repo.
And you can see here um if I go into the
server code. Well, first of all, in the
readme, if you want to run this server,
uh you can easily do so just by doing uh
let me see where my command is. Uh uv
run.
Uh it's just uvython server. py, which
will start up this server here that I
showed you the example of.
And if we go to the code, it's easiest
if I go to the align function, which
I'll increase in size here. So this
function here will simply align some
audio and text. It will take in some
audio. It will take in some text. True
or false as to whether to prever
preserve the original
words, their punctuation and
capitalization. A choice of aligner
defaulting to MMSFA, the torch audio
one. and then also language which is uh
needed as an input to the CTC align
library.
So first of all we will read in the
audio then we will take that audio and
we will convert it to a tensor. And so
here we just have an audio waveform and
this is going to be passed into the
alignment model which is a neural

Now the neural network has got a finite

And those chunks will all then generate

give for each uh audio seg each audio

of each character ABC is etc. So that's
the that's the um the emissions

And to see that you would have to go

the CTC one. So if I go to align audio,
it takes in the waveform, the sample

We're importing generate emissions. This
is the function that runs the model,
which can run on CUDA. And by the way,
CTC aligner, that library, when it
splits up your long waveform into
chunks, I think it's up to 30 seconds,
it will then run those as a batch to
make better use of the GPU. So there is
batching. when you make one request, it
will actually batch uh multiple parts of
that sequence and put them through at
once so that you get back out the
emissions for your whole long sequence
but in uh in pieces
once uh let's see. Yeah. So here you can

CTC supports multiple models. The models

here we're loading the CTC model.
We've got the waveform and then we're

We're also going to remove punctuation
but save that mapping. And once we have

the you can think of it like the the

And once we have those emissions,
we're going to be able to combine them

the alignment. So the way that you can
think of the alignment step which comes
after calculating emissions is that you

all the way along your audio. You also
have the ground truth text and so it's a

probabilities of the characters for the
audio.
So that's what's happening. Um when
you're getting the alignments, you're
feeding in the emissions and you're
feeding in the pre-processed uh tokens.
And by aligning the probability
distribution or rather by aligning the

likely uh probability sequence, you are
able to then get the alignment.
Once you have the alignment, you can get
the timing span for each of the words.
You can get the word timestamps.
You can then reverse the mapping and
then you can send this all back uh to
the LLM or sorry not to the LLM. You can
all set send this all back and that is
the alignment data that you need if
you're going to prepare your data for
doing some form of training for example.
So that's a very quick uh sketch of how
it works. I will just mention the models
that are being used. Um the English
model I'm using is Facebook wave tovecto
base 960h.
So I think that means it's fine-tuned on
960 hours or something like that. Uh
there are also Hindi and Arabic models.
I haven't fully tested out but you would

think even though it says English, it
will work fairly well for quite a few

certainly ones that use a similar um

And yeah, this is all done using uh
Mammud Araf's CTC force aligner library.
There's also torch audio which is a

tove base 960h model is permissively
licensed.
Okay, so that is pretty much it on the
demo and also the commercial demo. I'll
just show you now very briefly some of
the fundamentals on the theory of
alignment. I'll just illustrate a few
aspects here of how audio alignment
works.
So first thing I want to talk about is
emissions
emissions model
and then the second thing I'll talk
about is the alignment.
Okay.
So the what you start off with is some
text. It might be for example the and
then we go through normalization.
And that's just going to render it to
something like this here. TH like this.
Then we'll have some kind of a model
which will be a neural network. So it's
just a neural net. And we will take
audio.
So we'll have some audio wave. Uh in
fact maybe what I'll do is just
disconnect this here. Uh so we'll have
some input audio. Let's say it looks

It's not a great audio wave, but you get
the gist.
And the neural net here will output uh
some windows. So for every window,
well, first of all, it's going to have
some windows like this.
For each window, it's going to give some
probabilities of the different tokens.
So for example,
if I zoom in here for this first window,
it might say something like we have the
token t and that's going to be a
probability of uh let's say zero and
then we have a token well a character
space and that will be probability of
just for example and then on the next
window we might have t the probability
is 0.8 8 the probability of H is 0.2 and
then the next window we might have T the
probability is 0.2 the probability of a
space is 0.8 so this model at every
window is going to give a probability
for each of the possible
for each of the possible characters and
it's characters because we've got
normalized text coming in.
So now we we have this kind of map that
gives us the probabilities of each
character for each window. And what we
need to do is we need to take the ground
truth text which we know is thhe
and we basically need to figure out how
do we spread that amongst the windows
these windows here
so that it gives so that it best fits
the probability distribution.
And there's a way of doing this uh which
I've described more fully in the
advanced transcription repo but it's uh
I think I mispronounced it earlier
but it's a vurby method and what it does
is it basically steps forward through
the windows and considers what all of
the possible traces are. So for example
on the very first window the two
possibilities here are t and space. On
the second window, you've got T and H.
But the ways you can get to T and H are
either you could have TT. So in the
first window, you can have either T or
you can have space. In the second
window, you could have TT, you could
have space T, you could have space H.
Well, TH, and you could have space H.
There are all the four possibilities.
But we know already that the ground
truth is THE.
And because of that, we can already rule
out some of these. So this last one is
not possible because you can't have H
first. Also the TT is not possible
because you can't have two uh T's. So
the only possible ones we can consider
keeping with their probabilities are
these ones. So now we can go on to the
next window where we've t and space. And
in this case here we could have
underscore well just space TT. We could
have t HT. We could have
space t space and we can have t h space.
But again, we can rule out a lot of
these because they don't um respect the
actual outcome.
Uh so this one here is the only one
that's valid. And by the way, the space
is just it doesn't mean an actual space.
It means um just kind of a silence or
continuation. So actually this would be
considered a valid possibility to
continue because you have th and then
maybe the e will come in a later in a
later window here. So you can see by
moving from left to right and by ruling
out anything that's not possible you
will end up at the end with a number of
sequences that are remaining and you
will also end up with the probabilities
just by multiplying uh the probabilities
through the trees like this. So you
might have you know 0.2 2 0 1 0.3
and you will select uh the most likely
one. So this is how the alignment phase
works.
Now I'm getting into more uh academic
territory, but if you're interested in
how these models are trained, the
emissions model,
not the alignment model, this is
alignment what I'm talking about here.
But if you're interested in the
emissions model, and I spelled that with
two M's,
the way these are trained is actually on
unlabelled data, which is kind of
interesting. So you have a neural net,
and it takes in some audio.
So this is my audio representation here,
and it's going to spit out these uh
probabilities per window.
And the way that you train it is
you
will do it with unlabeled data.
And by unlabeled, I don't mean you you
don't have the text. You do have the
text. So you will have these
probabilities and you will have some
ground truth text
but the text will be will be unaligned.
So you'll have no timestamps.
So think of audio paired with ground
truth text that has no alignment.
And the way that you make this work is
the audio will output. It will output
again these kinds of uh probabilities
for each window.
And what you will do is you will keep.
So you will keep
any sequences
any sequences
that respect
the ground truth order
order.
So you may actually keep multiple
sequences. For example, if I have THE, I
might end up keeping space T space H
space E. I might also end up keeping
space H uh TH E. Uh I would also end up
keeping space space. These are all valid
sequences.
And by the way, I'm getting these just
by inspecting the emissions, by
inspecting the the output probabilities
for each character at each position. And
now it turns out, and this is maybe a
little surprising, if I just maximize
the sum of the probabilities
of these sequences,
if I just maximize the probabilities of
those specific sequences
and not the other ones that are invalid,
like say h would be invalid.
If I just maximize the ones that are
valid per the ground truth text order,
that actually gives a model that tends
to give very good alignments because
there often aren't that many valid
alignments the model will produce that
respect this order. So just by knowing
the correct order of the characters in
the ground truth text is actually enough
to be able to uh tune a base model. So
you take, as I said, a model that takes
in audio and gives out probabilities.
Well, you train it to do this. And this
is the loss function that you're going
to train it on in order for it to work.
Now, typically this is uh the
post-training. So you would post train a
model to do this. And in the
pre-training
what you're doing is you're taking a
model like wave tovec from from Facebook
which if I understand correctly it's um
it's trained through con contrastive and
also through masked loss. So for
example, they will take some audio in
the wild,
something like this,
and they may
feed in this to the model, and they will
get the model to then maybe predict part
of that wave. So you'll give it a full a
full signal, and you will get it to
predict the part here that has just been
masked out.
So it will try to model this and then
you'll do a reconstruction loss to grade

you get something like uh wave to vec.
This is the base model that you would

And now you train it to predict uh the

full_text: This is a video about aligning text and audio. It's relevant if you're training models such as texttospech models or speechtoext models, particularly if you want to have timestamps within transcripts you generate, for example, from whisper. It's also necessary for certain models that are token based like QTIs, texttospech, or speechtoext models. Last of all, if you're doing data preparation for training voice models, you often will want to chunk that data and you would like the chunks to respect sentence boundaries. Again, having timestamps, which means being able to align the audio and the text is important here if you want to do that. I'm going to show you a quick interface just to illustrate what I mean by alignment and some of the highle knobs you can turn and choices you might want to make when doing so. Then I'll show you how this integrates into a commercial application with Trellis Studio. And last of all, I'll cover a little bit of theory on how voice alignment works. Here is a quick demo that I have vibe coded called audio alignment. And it shows us how to align using two different models. It'll give you a feel for what alignment is and how it works. It's included in Trellis advanced transcription or advanced audio repository which you can find by going to trellles.com/advanced- audio. First thing I'll do here is just upload some voice. I've got a short recording. You can maybe listen to a short piece of it here. The Solar 10.7B is the fastest uh runner. Sorry, it's the it's uh this is me recording myself calling out some words in 2023, some LLM related words. And for convenience, I have connected up fireworks so we can quickly transcribe this. Okay, so I've transcribed the text here. Normally, I would make some corrections if I was going to train on this. Maybe I would put in some commas, for example, like this. I'm not going to be too worried because I mostly just want to align this with the audio. Now, there are two models that I have set up here. The first is MMS- FA. This is a default mod model from torch audio, although I understand it's going to be deprecated. And the second model is a commercially friend friendly model that's being run using the CTC aligner package. This one is uh openly licensed including for commercial use. So, I'll start off with the MMSFA and I'll click align. And in the background, the model is being loaded and the alignment is happening. So here's a sense. I'll just play it. And you can see that what we now have is the text is aligned with the audio. So let me play here. >> A strong it's a version of MRO 7B with extra layers. Claude 2.1 is the latest model from Enthropic. So you can see here we have uh the alignment of audio and text. And then we have a list of time stamps for the words. So we have the starting time stamp and the end. And given we have this, if we now do some form of sentence detection, say using full stops, we could detect uh sentences. If there were clean sentences, which there aren't cuz this is not necessarily clean text. But if we did have clean text, we would now also have word timestamps. And with the word timestamps and the sentence detection, we could create clean chunks that we can then use for training. And I'll show you an illustration of this a little bit later. Something to note, I can just run the uh commercially friendly one here as well. You'll notice the time it takes here to run the alignment. And you'll notice also that if I run it twice, it'll be faster because the second time we align, it has already cached the model on my Mac. This is all running locally and so it'll be faster. You can of course run these on GPUs as well. Something to appreciate is that alignment models, which I'll describe in more detail as to how they work, they operate on normalized text.
